feedback.text: First, I would like to express my support for the "stop killing games" initiative, I think this fits well within the context of this act. Second, I think more protections for consumers are a good thing especially in an increasingly digital world. Companies have increasingly shown they can only be trusted with one thing, increasing share-holder profit and not keeping their customers safe. So I support the extra protections proposed here and hope they are implemented in a way that they also protect us against new bad practices as the digital world often evolves quicker than lawmakers can write laws. For the remainder of my feedback I would like to express my disappointment in what I believe are violations of existing laws or laws which should have existed for a long time. The following examples are not acts of incredible journalism on my part. I've only started becoming more interested in protecting my online safety. These examples are very easy to find and not at all secretive and hidden wrong doings. Companies know they will get away with it with only a few days of bad PR, so they don't even bother anymore Roblox has been neglecting the safety of there users for a long time, https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_gXlauRB1EQ. Recently they took action against the rampant child predators on their platform. Not by taking action against them, but by taking action against an individual trying to against these predators. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JtctBNhS2wA With the UK abolishing online privacy for the sake of "protecting the kids", backed by Big Techs desire for more digital control, even going after a public good https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/cjr11qqvvwlo, I frankly find it insulting that such cases are left unpunished. Why should I trust that future protecting will actually be enforced. (Also worth mentioning in this context https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=C5RvoPQZQeM) Recently payment processors, visa and Mastercard, have been pressuring the removal of perfectly legal games, because it might damage their brand image. This is a broader trend that technology companies have gained to much power, to the point that they can act above the law. Anyone who actually listens knows that this is not what most consumers want, it is rather a small minority who is controlling what others can do. To round off, I fear that if we keep letting Big Tech overcontrol our lives and having paper thin protections, that this will lead to people having just 2 options: submit or risk serious harm in digital spaces the government can't reach.
